findings.title: CRITICAL_FIELDS still grades against the Tag 134-era field set — Tag 211l/219/220c fields never affect the grade
findings.description: The CRITICAL_FIELDS array enumerates 15 fields: meta.{sector,industry,ticker}, marketCap, revenue/margin metrics, annualRev/OpInc/FCF/Balance, revenueQ/opIncQ. None of the Tag 211l/Tag 219/Tag 220c additions are graded: annualSGA, annualDepreciation, annualShares, annualBalance[i].{currentAssets, currentLiabilities, totalLiabilities, accountsReceivable, netPPE}, metrics.{ebitda, enterpriseValue, beta, forwardPE}, timeseries.netIncomeQ. A snapshot that has every newer field nulled out still grades A+. Meanwhile pull-yahoo.js Tag 226a-2 (line 1240-1268) and Tag 230a (line 1291-1318) actively force full-pulls to backfill exactly these fields — proof that the codebase recognizes them as load-bearing. The grader is the only consumer that ignores them.
findings.mechanism: TOTAL_WEIGHT = 10x1.0 + 5x0.5 = 12.5. A snapshot missing ALL Tag 211l/219 newer fields but having all 15 legacy fields gets missingWeight=0, nanRatio=0, grade='A+'. Downstream beneish-m-score, ohlson-o-score, buyback-yield, sga-revenue-trend, working-capital-trend all return computable:false for that snapshot, but the grader signals everything is fine. F-DQ-002 (below) is the related field-coverage gap, but the impact differs: field-coverage drives drift-alerts, the grader drives the tier-cap (DATAQUALITY_ENFORCE) that influences which stocks become A/B picks. Anchor stocks (NVDA/MSFT/PLTR/META) all PASS today partly because they have the legacy fields populated, masking that 30%+ of the universe still lacks Tag 211l fields (Tag 225d probe showed 0/100 sampled snapshots had them at one point).
findings.reproducer: Pick any snapshot before its FTS cache expires (e.g. a pre-Tag-211l-cache stock that Tag 226a-2 currently rotates through schema-stale full-pulls). Inspect _quality.grade — it will read 'A' or 'A+' even though annual.annualSGA, annual.annualDepreciation, and annualBalance[i].currentAssets are absent. Score-aggregator with DATAQUALITY_ENFORCE=1 will then allow the stock to be A-tier picked despite ohlson-o-score reporting computable:false.
findings.impact: Grade-driven tier-cap is blind to ~6 newer fields used by 7+ methods. With DATAQUALITY_ENFORCE=1 a snapshot lacking every Tag 211l field still gets full evaluation. The recently-added schema-stale probe (Tag 226a-2) drives ~98% of the universe into full-pull because of these fields, yet the grade never reflects their absence — operators reading data-quality-report.md see A/B/C distribution that has no relationship to the methods' actual coverage. Compounds with F-DQ-003 (D-grade unreachable): the entire dq-enforcement path is effectively a no-op for new fields.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend CRITICAL_FIELDS to weight Tag 211l/219/220c additions: { id: 'annual.annualSGA>=2', weight: 0.5, check: s => _arrLen(s.annual && s.annual.annualSGA) >= 2 }, similar entries for annualDepreciation/annualShares, and for annualBalance[0].currentAssets/currentLiabilities (e.g. `s.annual.annualBalance[0] && Number.isFinite(s.annual.annualBalance[0].currentAssets)`). Also add metrics.enterpriseValue, metrics.ebitda, metrics.beta if betting-against-beta / ev-ebitda methods are considered structurally critical. Recalibrate GRADE_THRESHOLDS afterward to keep anchors A/A+. Document that each tier-tag bump that adds a field must add a corresponding CRITICAL_FIELDS entry — link to this finding in the Tag-bump checklist.

findings.title: TRACKED_FIELDS in field-coverage.js misses Tag 211l/Tag 219 fields — drift detector cannot observe Yahoo dropping the newer schema
findings.description: Tag 22's drift detector tracks coverage of 21 fields. None are Tag 211l (annualSGA, annualDepreciation, currentAssets, currentLiabilities, totalLiabilities, accountsReceivable, netPPE), Tag 219 (annualShares, beta, ebitda, enterpriseValue), or Tag 220c (targetMeanPrice, institutionsPercentHeld, earningsHistory). If Yahoo drops one of these fields tomorrow (as they dropped incomeStatementHistoryQuarterly in Nov 2024), nothing alerts. The pull-yahoo.js Tag 226a-2 probe (line 1240-1268) added a snapshot-level schema-stale detector for exactly Tag 211l, demonstrating that the project recognizes these fields as drift-relevant — but field-coverage.js was never extended.
findings.mechanism: computeCoverage iterates TRACKED_FIELDS and reports a coverage ratio per field. The drift detector compares current vs 14-run baseline AND vs an absolute 50% floor. Both branches require the field to be in TRACKED_FIELDS. A field that's not tracked simply does not exist in the alert pipeline — Yahoo could return zero annualSGA for the entire universe and field-coverage would report nothing.
findings.reproducer: Trace pull-yahoo's persisted snapshot to identify all stored fields (e.g. NVDA.json `annual` keys: annualRev, annualOpInc, annualNetIncome, annualGP, annualFCF, annualOCF, annualBalance, annualSBC, annualCapex, annualRnD, annualSGA, annualDepreciation, annualShares). Compare against TRACKED_FIELDS in field-coverage.js — the last three are absent. Manually null out `annual.annualSGA` across all snapshots, run detect-changes.js / field-coverage.computeCoverage — no drift signal.
findings.impact: Yahoo schema regressions on Tag 211l/Tag 219 fields are invisible. Beneish-m-score (Tag 209d), ohlson-o-score (Tag 210a), buyback-yield (Tag 219), betting-against-beta (Tag 219), sga-revenue-trend, working-capital-trend silently lose coverage with zero alerting. This is the SAME failure mode as the original Nov 2024 incomeStatementHistoryQuarterly regression that Tag 22 was designed to catch — Tag 22 just hasn't been updated for the newer fields. F-DQ-006 noted this for some fields (fcfMarginTTM, forwardPE, annualSBC, annualCapex, annualBalance, insiderOwnerPercent) — those were added, but the newer fields were never added.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to TRACKED_FIELDS: 'annual.annualSGA', 'annual.annualDepreciation', 'annual.annualShares', 'metrics.ebitda.value', 'metrics.enterpriseValue.value', 'metrics.beta.value', and a balance-row-aware path for 'annual.annualBalance[*].currentAssets' (requires extending getField/isPresent to inspect first row's named keys, or add a dedicated check). Add a regression test that loads anchor snapshots NVDA/MSFT/META/PLTR/COST and asserts every TRACKED_FIELDS entry returns coverage >= 0.95 — proves the tracking paths are not stale.

findings.title: Grade D is mathematically unreachable — the D-to-REJECT tier-cap is dead code
findings.description: The recalibrated GRADE_THRESHOLDS (F-DQ-007/F-DQ-008 recap) cap grade C at nanRatio <= 1.0 and put grade D at nanRatio > 1.0. But nanRatio = missingWeight / TOTAL_WEIGHT, where TOTAL_WEIGHT = sum of every field's weight. Even if EVERY critical field is missing, missingWeight = TOTAL_WEIGHT, so nanRatio = 1.0 exactly — which still satisfies the C condition (nanRatio <= 1.0). D is reachable only when nanRatio > 1.0, which is impossible by construction. The docstring at line 65 explicitly acknowledges this: 'D: > 100% = impossible (kept as safety net)' but the consequence is that tierCapForGrade('D') === 'REJECT' is dead code. No snapshot — not even one with `null` everywhere — can be REJECTed by the data-quality tier cap.
findings.mechanism: gradeSnapshot iterates CRITICAL_FIELDS and accumulates missingWeight. Maximum missingWeight = TOTAL_WEIGHT = 12.5. nanRatio = 12.5 / 12.5 = 1.0 exactly when everything is missing. Line 117: `else if (nanRatio <= GRADE_THRESHOLDS.C) grade = 'C';` — GRADE_THRESHOLDS.C = 1.0, so 1.0 <= 1.0 is TRUE, grade = 'C'. Line 118 (`else grade = 'D'`) is unreachable. The catch-all D path at line 99 (`if (!snapshot || typeof snapshot !== 'object')`) handles literal-falsy inputs but that's the only way to get D today.
findings.reproducer: Construct `gradeSnapshot({})` (an empty snapshot object): every check fails, missingWeight=12.5, nanRatio=1.0, grade='C'. Add any one field (e.g. `{meta: {sector: 'Tech'}}`): missingWeight=11.5, nanRatio=0.92, grade='C'. There is no snapshot shape that produces grade='D'.
findings.impact: tierCapForGrade('D') === 'REJECT' is dead code. The intended 'snapshot has too little data to score, reject' gate cannot fire. Snapshots with truly nothing (e.g. an FX-failure that wiped marketCap + all annual + all metrics) get tier-capped to NEAR_MISS instead of REJECT. With DATAQUALITY_ENFORCE=1 the worst-quality snapshots are still NEAR_MISS-eligible, polluting the watchlist quality tier. Combined with F-DQ-001 (newer fields not graded), this hollows out the entire dq-enforcement design.
findings.suggested_fix: Change GRADE_THRESHOLDS.C to a value strictly less than 1.0 (e.g. 0.85) and let D = `else` capture everything above 0.85. Or: reframe the boundary so D = nanRatio === 1.0 (only catches the everything-missing case). Or: tighten by replacing the threshold-table semantics — use weight-COUNT (number of fields missing) rather than weight ratio. The cleanest fix is `C: 0.85, D: implicit else` so a snapshot missing > ~85% of weight is REJECTed; that gives the dead code a purpose without breaking anchor grading.

findings.title: DATAQUALITY_ENFORCE silently fails open on the price-only refresh path (~80% of daily pulls)
findings.description: pull-yahoo.js Tag 166 price-only fast-path explicitly sets `existing._quality.grade = null` after refresh (line 1375) with the comment 'mark stale after price-only update'. score-aggregator.js gates the tier-cap behind `if (_dqEnabled && dataQuality && dataQuality.grade)` (line 365) — when grade is null, the entire dq-cap branch is skipped. Result: with DATAQUALITY_ENFORCE=1, only snapshots that just went through a full pull are gated; the ~80% that come through the daily price-only refresh have grade=null and fully bypass the cap. Operators who flip the flag thinking it gates all snapshots actually only gate ~20% of them.
findings.mechanism: Tag 166 (line 1374): `existing._quality.grade = null`. Engine reads stock._quality (line 256 score-aggregator.js: `ScoreAggregator.computeScore(..., stock && stock._quality)`). dataQuality = stock._quality (an object with grade=null, nanRatio=null, missingFields=null, staleSincePriceOnly=ISO). Inside computeScore: `if (_dqEnabled && dataQuality && dataQuality.grade)` — `dataQuality.grade` is null, fails truthy check, no tierCapForGrade call, no cap applied. dataQualityCapped stays false. The price-only stock's tier is unchanged regardless of its underlying data-quality.
findings.reproducer: Pre-flight: pick any anchor and inspect snapshot, confirm `_quality.grade` exists. Run pull-yahoo on a recent (<7d) snapshot to trigger the price-only path. Inspect output: `_quality.grade === null`, `_quality.staleSincePriceOnly` set. Run engine with DATAQUALITY_ENFORCE=1. Compare scoreResult.dataQualityCapped between a freshly full-pulled (will fire) and price-only-refreshed (won't fire) snapshot of the same C-grade stock.
findings.impact: DATAQUALITY_ENFORCE silently leaves 80% of the universe unprotected. A C-grade stock that gets price-only-refreshed daily for 7 days has grade=null for all 7 days and bypasses NEAR_MISS-cap. If/when Karl enables enforcement, expecting tier-cap protection, the actual protection covers only the 20% slice that ages out to full-pull on any given day. The Tag 229d-2 comment at score-aggregator line 371-378 about 'always flag the cap as applied' is moot when the cap never fires. F-DQ-001 (missing field tracking) and this finding compound: not only do new fields go ungraded, but the existing grades aren't even consulted for most snapshots.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) keep _quality.grade populated through price-only and just bump staleSincePriceOnly (the grade can't change without a fundamentals refresh, so why null it?), or (b) when dataQuality.grade is null AND dataQuality.staleSincePriceOnly is set, look up the previous grade from before the price-only refresh (preserve it in `_quality.lastFullPullGrade`), or (c) update score-aggregator's gate: when grade is null but a previous grade was recorded, apply that as a fallback. Cleanest is (a) — there's no semantic reason to invalidate the grade when only the price moved.

findings.title: data-quality-report.js and field-coverage.js both track metrics.insiderOwnerPercent — a field name that does not exist in the snapshot schema
findings.description: Both consumers track `metrics.insiderOwnerPercent` but pull-yahoo.js mapper writes the field as `metrics.insidersOwnership` (line 764: `insidersOwnership: _metric(_y(ks, 'heldPercentInsiders'), SRC, 0.7, asOf)`). Insider-ownership.js method (line 12) correctly reads `insidersOwnership`. Field-coverage's check `metrics.insiderOwnerPercent.value` returns undefined for every snapshot — coverage is permanently 0%, perpetually below the 50% ABSOLUTE_FLOOR, perpetually emitting a 'below-50pct-floor' HIGH alert that operators must learn to ignore. data-quality-report.md will always report 0% coverage for the 'insiderOwner' column.
findings.mechanism: getField(snapshot, 'metrics.insiderOwnerPercent.value') traverses snapshot.metrics.insiderOwnerPercent — undefined. Coverage = 0/N = 0. isPresent(undefined) = false. detectDrift: cur=0 < ABSOLUTE_FLOOR=0.5, push drift {field:'metrics.insiderOwnerPercent.value', severity:'HIGH', reason:'below-50pct-floor'}. Every run. The HIGH-severity alert teaches operators to ignore them (alert fatigue), which masks legitimate future drift signals on other fields.
findings.reproducer: Open any snapshot file (e.g. snapshots/NVDA.json). Search for 'insiderOwnerPercent' — zero hits. Search for 'insidersOwnership' — hit. Run `node scripts/data-quality-report.js` and check the report's insiderOwner row — '0%' every time. Run the field-coverage drift check — 'metrics.insiderOwnerPercent.value' appears in the drift list every run.
findings.impact: Permanent false-positive HIGH alert in field-coverage drift detection. The 'insiderOwner' column in data-quality-report.md is always 0%. Operators rationally start filtering 'metrics.insiderOwnerPercent.value' out of the drift list — and when a REAL field (e.g. annualSBC) drifts the same way, the muscle memory of 'this is the bogus alert' may suppress action. Compounds with F-DQ-002 (other missing fields) — the alert pipeline already has noise it shouldn't have.
findings.suggested_fix: Rename to `metrics.insidersOwnership.value` (the actual canonical key) in both files. field-coverage.js line 41 and line 18 comment; data-quality-report.js line 46. Add a unit test that loads NVDA snapshot fixture and asserts isPresent(getField(snapshot, FIELD)) === true for every TRACKED_FIELD entry — guarantees no future name drift can ship.

findings.title: data-quality-report.js array-presence uses naive `x != null` filter — counts {value: null} envelopes as present
findings.description: KEY_FIELDS at lines 39-44 use `.filter(x => x != null).length >= N` to count present entries. Tag 206f (F-DP-030/031) preserves `{value: null}` envelopes as positional placeholders to keep annualRev[i] aligned with annualOpInc[i]. A `{value: null}` object is `!= null` (the OBJECT is not null), so it counts as present in this filter. data-quality.js _arrLen has the explicit fix for this (lines 83-89: `if (typeof x === 'object' && 'value' in x) return Number.isFinite(x.value)`), and field-coverage.js isPresent does too (lines 69-74). data-quality-report.js was missed during the F-DQ-004 / F-DQ-005 fix wave.
findings.mechanism: An annualOpInc array for a bank like [null, {value: 5e9}, null, {value: 4e9}, null] — five entries, only two with finite values. `.filter(x => x != null).length` returns 2 (filters out raw nulls only, keeps {value: null} envelopes — though in this example all envelope wrappers have finite values so the bug doesn't manifest). The actual failure mode: annualRev = [{value: null}, {value: 100}, null] — `.filter(x => x != null).length` = 2 because the {value: null} survives the filter. But only 1 entry has actual revenue data. Coverage row 'annualRev>=3' would PASS for [{value:null}, {value:null}, {value:null}] (3 envelopes survive, length=3 >= 3) even though zero actual revenue is present.
findings.reproducer: Build a synthetic snapshot with `annual.annualRev = [{value: null}, {value: null}, {value: null}, {value: 100e9}]` — call data-quality-report's KEY_FIELDS['annualRev>=3'].get(s) — returns true (4 != null entries pass filter, all 4 >= 3). Now call methods/data-quality.js gradeSnapshot on the same snapshot — _arrLen filters to 1 (only the value=100e9 entry has finite value), so 'annual.annualRev>=3' check fails, missingWeight += 1.0. The two reports DISAGREE on the same snapshot.
findings.impact: data-quality-report.md systematically overstates coverage of array-typed fields. Reported 'annualRev>=3' coverage will look higher than the grader's actual measure of usable revenue data. Operators see a green 92% in the report and conclude annualRev is healthy when ~5-10% of those snapshots are {value:null} envelopes that no method can score on. Discrepancy with gradeSnapshot's own counts is itself a confidence-eroder when audits cross-check.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the inline filter with a shared helper from data-quality.js: `const { _arrLen } = require('../methods/data-quality.js')` (export it) and use `_arrLen(s.annual && s.annual.annualRev) >= 3`. Single source of truth, can never drift again. Same for annualFCF, annualBalance, annualSBC, annualCapex, revenueQ entries.

findings.title: sector-medians-compute reads only annualBalance[0]/annualNetIncome[0] — Tag 206f positional-null preservation silently drops stocks from sector buckets
findings.description: gatherBySubProfile reads `stock.annual.annualNetIncome[0] && stock.annual.annualNetIncome[0].value` (and similar for annualOpInc/annualFCF/annualBalance). Tag 206f / F-DP-030 deliberately preserves `null` placeholders at the front of these arrays to maintain positional alignment with annualRev[i]. For a stock whose latest annual report is missing OpInc (common for fintech/banks: Yahoo's FTS omits operatingIncome for that year), annualOpInc[0] = null, the `&&` short-circuits, oi = null, the stock contributes nothing to the roce bucket — even though annualOpInc[1] is a perfectly valid prior-year value. The latest-year-null pattern silently excludes the stock from all four metric buckets, biasing sector medians toward names with complete latest-year data.
findings.mechanism: Tag 206f preserves null entries so annualRev[i], annualOpInc[i], annualNetIncome[i], annualFCF[i] all refer to the same fiscal year. A bank where Yahoo returned annualOpInc=[null, {value:5e9}, {value:4.5e9}, null] (Tag 203 fallback didn't activate or only filled some years) reads `annualOpInc[0]` = null at line 70, oi = null. Stock excluded from globalBuckets.{spId}.roce.push and regionalBuckets[region][spId].roce.push. Same path for annualNetIncome[0] (roic) and annualFCF[0] (fcf-yield) and annualBalance[0] (used as denominator). Across a 15k universe, even a 5-10% prevalence of latest-year-null produces a measurable survivor-bias drift in computed sector medians toward stocks with cleaner latest-year fundamentals.
findings.reproducer: Synthesize stock with `annual.annualNetIncome = [null, {value: 1e9}]` and `annual.annualBalance = [{totalAssets: 50e9, totalCash: 5e9}, ...]`. Run gatherBySubProfile([stock], classify) — globalBuckets[spId].roic is empty (ni is null because of the first-entry-null short-circuit). Now retry with annualNetIncome=[{value: 1e9}, null] — globalBuckets[spId].roic is populated. Same data shape (one latest, one prior — just different position) produces different bucket inclusion.
findings.impact: Sector medians (roic/roce/fcf-yield) systematically biased toward stocks with complete latest-year fundamentals. Methods using sector-median lookup (gross-profitability, quality-compounder-roic, sector-relative-roic, magic-formula) compare candidates against an artificially-elevated bar — stocks at the survivor-bias bucket level look 'merely average' instead of 'top quartile'. F-ME-008 fix (positive-only median for ROIC/ROCE) partly mitigates by excluding losses but doesn't address the null-bias. The effect is small per-stock (5-10%) but compounds across 15k tickers and affects every sector-relative ranking. F-DP-030 fixed the alignment side; this is the unfixed consumer side.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `[0]` reads with a `firstFinite` helper: `const ni = _firstFinite(stock.annual.annualNetIncome)` where `_firstFinite(arr) = arr?.find(x => x != null && typeof x === 'object' && Number.isFinite(x.value))?.value ?? null`. For annualBalance use `_firstNonNullRow(stock.annual.annualBalance, ['totalAssets', 'totalCash'])`. Alternatively, document that the medians use 'most recent populated year' — and apply uniformly. This also handles the inverse case (stock with stale latest year but valid prior — currently silently excluded).

findings.title: data-quality-report.js gradeSnapshot loader writes a recoverable WARN but the fallback returns grade='unknown' — gradeCounts table silently shows all zeros
findings.description: If the require of methods/data-quality.js fails for any reason (syntax error, require cycle, dependency missing), the loader installs a stub `gradeSnapshot = () => ({ grade: 'unknown' })`. The report then iterates all snapshots and increments `gradeCounts[grade]`. gradeCounts is initialized to `{ 'A+': 0, A: 0, B: 0, C: 0, D: 0, unknown: 0 }` (line 101). With the stub, every snapshot becomes 'unknown', gradeCounts.unknown = N, others stay 0. The markdown report (lines 151-155) ONLY writes table rows for 'A+', 'A', 'B', 'C', 'D' — there is no row for 'unknown'. So an operator viewing the report sees `Grade A+: 0 (0%)` / `Grade A: 0 (0%)` / ... and concludes the universe has zero gradeable stocks — but they have no way to discover WHY without re-reading the CI log for the loader WARN. F-SC-032's WARN is technically printed but is buried among potentially thousands of other log lines.
findings.mechanism: require('../methods/data-quality.js') throws. catch installs stub. main() loops snapshots, calls `gradeSnapshot(s)`, returns {grade:'unknown',...}. `gradeCounts[grade] = (gradeCounts[grade] || 0) + 1`, gradeCounts.unknown becomes N. Lines 151-155 emit rows only for A+/A/B/C/D — all read as 0, total snapshots N. Operator reading the report sees 'Total snapshots: 15,734 / Grade A+: 0 (0%) / ... / Grade D: 0 (0%)' — concludes grader is broken or universe is empty. The 'unknown' bucket is computed but never displayed.
findings.reproducer: Temporarily break methods/data-quality.js (e.g. add a syntax error). Run `node scripts/data-quality-report.js`. Inspect outputs/data-quality-report.md — Summary table shows 0 across all grades despite 15k+ snapshots loaded. Per-exchange table shows 0/0/0/0/0 columns. There is no indication of WHY.
findings.impact: If the grader module ever breaks (e.g. as part of an unrelated refactor or an upgrade-day mistake), the data-quality report shows all-zeros across the grade tier columns — a state indistinguishable from 'every snapshot is grade unknown'. The WARN on stderr is the only diagnostic, easy to miss in CI logs. Combined with F-DQ-005 (the report already has a permanent 0% column from insiderOwner name drift), operators stop trusting near-zero numbers in the report — masking real regressions.
findings.suggested_fix: (a) Add 'unknown' to the grade table rows when count > 0 — at least the failure is visible. (b) When loader fails, also write a stub line to the markdown report itself: `> WARNING: data-quality grader failed to load (<error>). All snapshots reported as 'unknown'.` Then operators see the failure inline. (c) Optionally, exit non-zero in CI when the grader stub is in use AND any snapshot tried to grade — promotes silent failure to loud failure.

findings.title: Inside a single ADR snapshot, price.regularMarketPrice is FX-scaled by financial currency while targetMeanPrice/targetMedianPrice stay in trading currency — same-snapshot cross-field comparisons silently wrong
findings.description: F-DP-002 documents that _priceOnlyUpdate scales regularMarketPrice and marketCap by `meta.fxRateApplied` (the financial-currency factor). F-DP-004 documents that targetMeanPrice / targetMedianPrice are NOT in CCY_DENOMINATED_METRICS so they remain in trading currency after full-pull. Within a single ADR snapshot for TSM (financial=TWD, trading=USD), this means after the next price-only refresh: existing.price.regularMarketPrice = USD-quote * TWD-rate (0.031) ~ 32x too small; existing.metrics.targetMeanPrice.value = unchanged USD analyst target. analyst-upside-style methods that compute `(targetMean - currentPrice) / currentPrice` then divide a USD price-target by a 32x-too-small 'current price', producing 32x upside computed from a ratio of two numbers in inconsistent units. F-DP-002 covered the corruption per se; this finding is the second-order downstream: the same snapshot has internally inconsistent units that any cross-field method silently fails on.
findings.mechanism: Full pull for TSM: rcOriginal='TWD', tradingCurrency='USD'. _convertSnapshotToUSD scales marketCap, revenueTTM, annual.*, timeseries.* by factor=0.031 (TWD-to-USD). targetMeanPrice and targetMedianPrice are NOT in CCY_DENOMINATED_METRICS, stay at their raw Yahoo value, which IS USD for TSM (Yahoo returns price targets in trading currency for ADRs). marketCap goes from $1.1T USD to $34B post-scaling (F-DP-005 / F-DP-002). targetMeanPrice stays at ~$200 USD. Now ratio targetMeanPrice/marketCap mixes apples (USD target) and a 1/32-scaled apple (USD-cap-shrunken-by-TWD-factor). Any heuristic that combines targetMean and marketCap (e.g. analyst-target-implied-cap vs current-cap delta) produces a 30x wrong number. The price-only path (line 1345) makes the corruption WORSE on each refresh: existing.price.regularMarketPrice = q.regularMarketPrice (USD) * 0.031 = $6.20-ish for a real $200 stock.
findings.reproducer: Pull TSM with full path. Inspect TSM.json: marketCap.value ~ 3.4e10 (should be ~ 1.1e12). metrics.targetMeanPrice.value ~ 200 (USD analyst target). Trigger price-only on the same ticker. Inspect price.regularMarketPrice — will be ~6.20 not ~200. Now any 'upside' calc using targetMeanPrice / price gives 200/6.2 ~ 32x of real, plotting analyst-upside as +3000% instead of (e.g.) +5%.
findings.impact: F-DP-002 already flagged the floor-induced delete risk and 32x corruption. This finding extends: even WHEN the snapshot survives the $1B floor, the snapshot is internally currency-inconsistent — same-record fields mix two different units. Any current or future analyst-upside / fair-value-vs-cap method on ADRs reads broken inputs and silently emits broken outputs. ADRs affected: TSM, BABA, BIDU, JD, NU, 9988.HK, ASML, NVO and every other DR. With Tag 220c surfacing targetMeanPrice into snapshots (no method consumes it today, but the dashboard pre-feeds), the inconsistency is live data lake pollution.
findings.suggested_fix: Two-step. (i) Store `meta.tradingFxRateApplied` alongside `meta.fxRateApplied` (set tradingFxRateApplied = FX_TO_USD[tradingCurrency] in _convertSnapshotToUSD). (ii) In CCY_DENOMINATED_METRICS, split into two arrays: financial-ccy metrics (revenueTTM, fcfTTM, ebitda, enterpriseValue, annual.*, timeseries.*) and trading-ccy metrics (targetMeanPrice, targetMedianPrice, price.regularMarketPrice, marketCap if Yahoo returns it in trading ccy for ADRs — empirically verify per F-DP-005). Scale each set by its respective rate. Extends F-DP-002 / F-DP-004 fixes without duplicating; the proposed unification of trading-vs-financial FX handling solves both classes of corruption.

files_examined: pull-yahoo.js (currency normalization + mapper sections only: _convertSnapshotToUSD lines 247-347, _priceOnlyUpdate 1320-1385, _ftsExtractByYear/mapFTS* 916-1030, schema-stale probes 1240-1318, mapper 473-888)
files_skipped: pull-yahoo.js pulling-logic (worker pool, retry, manifest write — out of scope per task brief; covered by data-pipeline.json)
methods/sector-medians-compute.js percentile + writeRollingMedians (out of scope; only input-validation slice examined)
